coredata.dc:title: A Cu2+-assisted fluorescence switch biosensor for detecting of coenzyme A employing nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Fengzhou
Tang, Huaying
Yu, Jianhua
Ge, Jia
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a simple and sensitive Cu2+-assisted fluorescence switch biosensor for the detection of coenzyme A (CoA) was proposed by employing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). N-CDs were successfully synthesized by sodium alginate and melatonin via pyrolysis. The as-prepared N-CDs were spherical with an average diameter of 2.8 nm and exhibited blue emission (λem = 480 nm, λex = 360 nm) with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 50.2%. The intense blue emission of the N-CDs could be effectively quenched by copper ions through the formation of the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex. With the introduction of CoA, a more stable CoA/Cu2+ complex formed, leading to the fluorescence recovery of N-CDs. Based on this strategy, CoA could be sensitively and selectively detected with a good linear relationship in the range of 0.02–5.00 μM and with a detection limit of 12 nM. In addition, this sensor was applied for CoA detection in human serum samples with satisfactory recovery. The results showed great potential towards advancing applications in CoA-dependent bioresearch.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrogen-doped
Coenzyme A
Copper ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility investigation of the sensing strategy
Detection of CoA in human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of N-CDs. (A) TEM image of N-CDs. (B) Particle size distribution of N-CDs. (C) XRD patterns and (D) Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of N-CDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of the (A) full range, (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, and (D) O 1s peaks of the N-CDs.

The optical properties of N-CDs. (A) The excitation spectra (λem = 480 nm, green line) and emission spectra (λex = 360 nm, blue line) of the N-CDs. The inset shows the photos of aqueous dispersions of as-prepared N-CDs under UV (365 nm) irradiation. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (340–425 nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Verification of feasibility analysis. The fluorescence emission spectra of different sensing systems: (a) N-CDs; (b) N-CDs/Cu2+; (c) N-CDs/Cu2+ + CoA.

Detection of CoA in buffer solution. (A) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex in the presence of different concentrations of CoA (from bottom to top, 0, 0.02, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 5, and 10 μM). (B) Calibration curve of the assay system for CoA detection. The inset shows the linear response between 0.02 and 5 μM CoA.

Selectivity of this sensor for the CoA assay. Fluorescence response of the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex to various biomolecules (the concentrations of AA, UA, glucose, NADP, and ATP are 100 μM, and the concentration of CoA is 10 μM).
Schematic illustration of pyrolysis synthesis of N-CDs and the N-CDs/Cu2+ fluorescence switch biosensor for CoA detection.

head.ce:title: A Cu2+-assisted fluorescence switch biosensor for detecting of coenzyme A employing nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental and Biological Engineering, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Ecology-Toxicological Effects & Control for Emerging Contaminants, Putian University, Putian, 351100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental and Biological Engineering
Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Ecology-Toxicological Effects & Control for Emerging Contaminants

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental and Biological Engineering, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Ecology-Toxicological Effects & Control for Emerging Contaminants, Putian University, Putian, 351100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Green Catalysis Center, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Green Catalysis Center, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a simple and sensitive Cu2+-assisted fluorescence switch biosensor for the detection of coenzyme A (CoA) was proposed by employing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). N-CDs were successfully synthesized by sodium alginate and melatonin via pyrolysis. The as-prepared N-CDs were spherical with an average diameter of 2.8 nm and exhibited blue emission (λem = 480 nm, λex = 360 nm) with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 50.2%. The intense blue emission of the N-CDs could be effectively quenched by copper ions through the formation of the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex. With the introduction of CoA, a more stable CoA/Cu2+ complex formed, leading to the fluorescence recovery of N-CDs. Based on this strategy, CoA could be sensitively and selectively detected with a good linear relationship in the range of 0.02–5.00 μM and with a detection limit of 12 nM. In addition, this sensor was applied for CoA detection in human serum samples with satisfactory recovery. The results showed great potential towards advancing applications in CoA-dependent bioresearch.

In this work, based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), a sensitive, selective, Cu2+ -assisted fluorescent switch biosensor for the detection of coenzyme A was developed. Furthermore, the biosensor was applied for CoA detection in human serum samples.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Coenzyme A (CoA), a ubiquitous and important coenzyme in all living cells, is composed of pantothenic acid (B5 vitamin), adenosine diphosphate and β-mercaptoethylamine. Its main functions are to activate and transfer acyl groups [1,2]. As substrates or intermediates, CoA and thioester derivatives are involved in numerous metabolic pathways, including the metabolism of the tricarboxylic acid cycle (TCA), as well as fatty acid synthesis and oxidation [3]. The levels of CoA and its derivatives are associated with physiological and pathological conditions, and they are related to many biological events, such as neurodegeneration, protein acetylation, epigenetics and signal transduction [4]. The clinical symptoms of CoA deficiency include fatigue, irritability, inappetence, convulsion, anxiety, and numbness [5]. Clinically, CoA is used as an adjuvant medicine to promote the metabolism of carbohydrates, lipids, and amino acids [6]. Therefore, the monitoring of CoA is essential for the timely and accurate diagnosis of diseases.

Since the discovery of CoA by Lipmann and coworkers in the early 1950s, multifarious methods have been established to assay CoA [7]. The traditional methods for CoA and acetyl-CoA detection include thin layer chromatography, enzymatic assays, capillary electrophoresis, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), and electrochemical methods [8–14]. These methods have their own advantages as well as defects. For example, enzymatic assays have merits of high selectivity, but they are also time-consuming and require large amounts of sample. Simultaneous detection of multiple CoA species can be realized by HPLC and LC-MS; however, they require expensive equipment, or lack of sensitivity and selectivity without derivatization or radiolabeling of CoA and derivatives. Recently, combining nanomaterials, such as AuNPs, CdTe QDs, and MOFs [15–18], a series of new methods were applied to the analysis of CoA. However, the synthesis of nanomaterials is complicated. Hence, the development of a simple, reliable and sensitive method for CoA detection is of great significance in clinical and CoA-dependent bioresearch.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new kind of fluorescent nanomaterials that were first described in 2004 by the Scrivens group and were first named “carbon dots” in 2006 by Sun and coworkers [19,20]. Due to the unique optical and chemical properties of CDs, such as superior anti-photobleaching, tunable photoluminescence properties, good water solubility, high chemical stability, ease of preparation, excellent biocompatibility and environmental friendliness [21–24], CDs have attracted considerable attention and have been widely used in medical diagnosis, catalysis, drug delivery, energy sources, biochemical sensors, and bioimaging [25–33]. However, most of the early reported CDs showed a relatively low fluorescent quantum yield, e.g., the quantum yield of CDs in the first work was only 1.6% [19]. In 2012, Wang et al. first developed a new nitrogen-doped CD (N-CD) using glycerol and melamine as precursors, and the quantum yield of the N-CDs increased to 22% [34]. This finding confirmed that heteroatom doping is an effective strategy to improve the properties of CDs. Until now, many single- or multi-atoms doped-CDs have been reported, such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, boron and metal atoms (Cu, Zn, Mn, etc.) [35]. In particular, nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) are a typical kind of heteroatom-doped CDs with the most widespread application. Carbonization, hydrothermal methods, and ultrasonic- and microplasma-assisted methods have been explored to prepare N-CDs [36–38]. However, the quantum yield of a large proportion of N-CDs is lower than 40%. Choosing an appropriate combination of C and N precursors is one of the common methods to further improve the quantum yield of N-CDs.

In this work, we first presented a facile and general strategy to prepare N-CDs with high quantum yield using sodium alginate and melatonin as precursors. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited blue emission (λem = 480 nm) under UV (λex = 360 nm) illumination with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 50.2% (for details, see the supporting information). Furthermore, based on the as-prepared N-CDs and with the assistance of Cu2+, a fluorescence switch biosensor for the detection of CoA was explored. As shown in Scheme 1 , when a certain amount of Cu2+ was added to the N-CDs solution, owing to the affinity between Cu2+ and amines on the surface of the N-CDs, an N-CDs/Cu2+ complex formed. The fluorescence of N-CDs could be directly quenched by Cu2+ through electron or energy transfer between Cu2+ and N-CDs [39–41]. Interestingly, with the addition of CoA in N-CDs/Cu2+ solution, we found that the fluorescence of quenched N-CDs could be selectively restored due to the competitive combination of CoA with Cu2+. According to this phenomenon, the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex was used as the probe for the detection of CoA. In addition, this fluorescence switch biosensor was further successfully applied to detect CoA in human serum samples.

Sodium alginate, melatonin, ascorbic acid (AA), coenzyme A (CoA), uric acid (UA), glucose, nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP), and ATP were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used as received without additional purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (Millipore, 18.2 MΩ cm @25 °C). Human serum from volunteers was collected by the First Affiliated Hospital of Zhengzhou University, and informed consent was obtained for the use of human serum.

UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were taken using a Cary Bio-100 UV/Vis spectrometer (Varian). Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs were measured on an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm and an emission wavelength range of 450–600 nm (max λem = 480 nm). Both the excitation and emission slits were set at 2.5 nm, with a 0.2 s response time at 700 V PMT voltage. A BRUKER TENSOR 27 spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) was employed to collect the Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. Images (TEM) were obtained by transmission electron microscope (JEOL Model JEM-2010F) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The surface composition and chemical state of the product were analyzed with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi).

The N-CDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of sodium alginate and melatonin. Briefly, melatonin (2.44 g) and sodium alginate (1.28 g) were mixed evenly added to a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. Then, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. The resultant dark brown product was dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol. The yellow-brown product was collected by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through microporous membranes to remove the large carbon products. Finally, the N-CDs were obtained with dialysis (Mw: 1000 Da) for 24 h and stored light-free at 4 °C.

Typically, the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex was prepared by diluting 10 μL of N-CDs solution (0.2 mg/mL) and 20 μL of Cu2+ ion (50 μM) solution with 60 μL of PB buffer solution (100 mM, pH 7.4 @25 °C). For CoA detection, 10 μL aliquots of CoA solutions with different concentrations were added to the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex solution, and the mixture solution was allowed to react at room temperature for 10 min before fluorescence measurement. To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method, a series of potential interferences (AA, UA, glucose, NADP, ATP) were added to the detection system, and the other detection procedures were consistent with the above procedures.

For CoA detection in complex biofluid samples, human serum samples were treated by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. A 1% human serum solution was prepared by diluting supernatant with PB buffer. The 1% human serum solution was used instead of clean PB buffer solution in the following detection procedures. Different final concentrations of CoA (50, 100, 200 nM) were added into the 1% human serum solution containing the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex, and the other procedures were the same as those shown in the above experiments for CoA detection in buffer.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to identify the size and morphology of the as-prepared N-CDs. TEM indicated that the N-CDs were spherical in shape (Fig. 1 A) and wel-dispersed, with an average diameter of 2.8 nm (Fig. 1B). Additionally, a typical X-ray diffraction analysis confirmed that the N-CDs were in the amorphous phase (2θ = 22.92°, Fig. 1C). The chemical structure of the N-CDs was analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and several characteristic absorption peaks were observed, as shown in Fig. 1D, including a C–N stretching vibration at 1162 cm−1, a C–O bending vibration at 1498 cm−1, an N–H bending vibration at 1622 cm−1, an N–H stretching vibration at 3241 cm−1, and an intense and wide O–H peak at 3346 cm−1. The hydroxyl and amino groups conferred excellent water solubility on the N-CDs. In addition, XPS analysis was conducted to identify the surface composition and chemical environments of the N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the XPS spectrum of the N-CDs presented three major peaks at 284.8, 399.62 and 532.69 eV, which correspond to the elements of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. Furthermore, high-resolution spectra were used to reveal the chemical environments of C1s, N1s and O1s. As shown in Fig. 2B, C and D, C1s contained C–C, C–N/C–O and CO groups at 284.79 eV, 286.32 eV and 288.54 eV, respectively. C–N–C (399.59 eV) and N–H (400.59 eV) were the main components of N1s. The O1s exhibited two components at 531.07 eV and 532.72 eV that were attributed to CO and C–OH/C–O–C. The results indicated that the N-CDs were mainly composed of C, N and O and were rich in amino groups on the surfaces, which responded to the FT-IR spectrum.

To investigate the optical properties of the N-CDs, as shown in Fig. 3 A, fluorescent spectra of the as-prepared N-CDs were obtained. With the maximum excitation at 360 nm, the N-CDs exhibited a blue fluorescence emission peak at 480 nm. In addition, a series of fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CDs were recorded with different excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 nm to 425 nm. Different from most excitation-dependent CDs, the as-prepared N-CDs appeared excitation-dependent in stages. As displayed in Fig. 3B, with an excitation wavelength between 340 and 380 nm, the emission peak was centered at approximately 470–480 nm, which endowed the N-CD solution with bright blue fluorescence under UV light. As the excitation wavelength was ≥390 nm, the emission spectra obviously redshifted with increasing excitation wavelength. It is speculated that this excitation-dependent characteristic may be due to the size heterogeneity and the N-doping of the N-CDs [42]. Furthermore, by using dansyl chloride as a standard, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the as-prepared N-CDs was calculated to be 50.2%, which was higher than the other precursors-mediated synthesis of N-CDs [37,38,43].

In addition, to study the photostability of N-CDs, the resistance to pH, ionic strength and illumination were investigated. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence of the N-CDs was stable between 7.0 and 9.0. However, with increasing acidity (pH < 7.0) and alkalinity (pH > 9.0), the fluorescence decreased slightly. For the effect of ionic strength, sodium chloride was used as the model salt. As Fig. S2 shows, with increasing NaCl concentration from 0 to 1000 mM, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs remained unaltered, demonstrating that the N-CDs have extreme resistance to high-ionic strength solutions. Additionally, the N-CDs showed excellent anti-photobleaching properties, as the N-CDs were continuously illuminated under a UV lamp (365 nm) for 60 min, and the decay rate of fluorescence intensity was less than 5% (Fig. S3). In summary, the results revealed that the as-prepared N-CDs possessed excellent photostability, which is a critical factor in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility investigation of the sensing strategy
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate our sensing strategy, feasibility studies under different conditions were performed. As shown in Fig. 4 , the N-CD solution exhibited strong blue fluorescence under excitation at 360 nm (curve a). With the addition of Cu2+ ion, the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex formed, and an effective electron or energy transfer process occurred between Cu2+ ions and N-CDs, resulting in a significant decrease in the fluorescence of the N-CDs (curve b). However, as the CoA was introduced, owing to the high affinity of CoA to Cu2+, CoA could usurp Cu2+ from the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex, leading to fluorescence recovery of the N-CDs (curve c). The results demonstrated that this sensing strategy based on a Cu2+-assisted fluorescence switch biosensor for CoA detection was reliably feasible.

To achieve the best sensing sensitivity for CoA detection, optimization of the detection conditions was performed. First, the background fluorescence was a pivotal factor in sensing sensitivity in this method. To obtain a high ratio of signal to background for sensing, the concentration of Cu2+ was investigated. As Fig. S4 shows, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs decreased gradually with increasing Cu2+ concentration, and the maximum quenching efficacy was approximately 90% upon the addition of ≥10 μM Cu2+ ions. Thus, 10 μM Cu2+ was chosen in the following experiments. Second, the reaction time of CoA and the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex was an important factor affecting the fluorescence recovery rate of the N-CDs and the testing period. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs increased rapidly upon the addition of CoA and reached equilibrium within 10 min. Considering the detection period, 10 min was chosen as the optimal reaction time of CoA and the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex.

Under optimization of the detection experimental conditions, a series of samples with different concentrations of CoAfrom 0–10 μM were measured to test the quantitative assay ability of this N-CD-based biosensor. As shown in the fluorescence spectra of Fig. 5 A, with increasing CoA concentrations, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs increased gradually. A good linear correlation emerged between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of CoA in the range from 0.02 to 5 μM (R2 = 0.9929, Fig. 5B). The detection limit of CoA was calculated to be 12 nM according to the 3σ rule, which was comparable to or lower than those of the other CoA sensors listed in Table S1. These results proved that the N-CD-based fluorescence sensor has considerable potential as a simple, quantitative and sensitive strategy for the CoA assay.

The specificity of the biosensor for the CoA assay was investigated by using AA, UA, glucose, NADP, and ATP as controls. The results shown in Fig. 6 show that as 10 μM CoA was added, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced approximated 7 times higher than that of the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex. However, no obvious changes in the fluorescence intensity were observed when AA, UA, glucose, NADP, and ATP were added separately, even though the concentrations were 10 times higher than that of CoA. Furthermore, as predicted, the fluorescence recovery of the mixture group (CoA and other nontarget biomolecules) was equivalent to that of the CoA group. This revealed that the recognition between CoA and Cu2+ was highly selective and did not interfere with other interfering biomolecules. Thus, the results suggested the high selectivity of the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex for CoA analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CoA in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the sensing ability of this biosensor in a complex biofluid, the detection of CoA in 1% human serum samples was investigated. Different final concentrations of CoA (50, 100, 200 nM) were spiked into 1% human serum samples, and measured as mentioned above. As listed in Table S2, recoveries of CoA for determination in human serum samples ranged from 96.4% to 106.4%. These results indicated that the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex-based fluorescent sensing strategy had high specificity for the detection of CoA in complex biofluid samples, suggesting its good potential for biomedical applications.

In summary, highly fluorescent N-CDs have been synthesized via a one-step pyrolysis method by using sodium alginate and melatonin as precursors. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibit good dispersibility, photostability, and bright blue fluorescence with a high quantum yield of 50.2%. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs can be efficiently quenched by Cu2+ ions with the formation of the N-CDs/Cu2+ complex. The N-CDs/Cu2+ complex was applied for sensitive and selective sensing of CoA via Cu2+ ion and CoA interaction. The linear range of the fluorescent detection of CoA was 0.02–5.00 μM, with an LOD of 12 nM. The prepared biosensor detected CoA from human serum samples with high recovery. In view of these advantages, such a simple, fast and sensitive fluorescence sensor is feasible for the CoA assay and CoA-dependent research.

Fengzhou Xu: Data curation, Writing - original draft preparation, Writing-Review and Editing, Visualization, Investigation, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Huaying Tang: Software, Data curation, Writing-Reviewing and Editing. Jianhua Yu: Software, Data curation, Writing- Reviewing and Editing. Jia Ge: Writing - original draft preparation, Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Start-up Project of Putian University (2019116); Education and scientific research project for young and middle-aged teachers of Fujian Province (JAT190569); the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2020J05209); Program for New Century Excellent Talents in Fujian Province University; the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21505122).
